responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s 2026–2029 digital strategy demands reduced reliance on foreign platform vendors while ensuring continuity of essential public services. The current MitID ecosystem’s dependence on Apple and Google for app distribution, integrity signals, and telemetry creates a single‑point failure risk and limits procurement flexibility. Balancing speed of adoption, cost of certification, and resilience of identity services requires a coordinated approach that blends policy framing, technical proof‑of‑concept, coalition building, and EU‑level standards pressure. The selected levers target these tensions by redefining procurement language, demonstrating platform‑neutral authentication, engaging stakeholders, and aligning national requirements with European interoperability goals.

levers.name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reduces reliance on Apple and Google by diversifying platform integrity signals, but it may increase integration overhead and delay procurement cycles. It forces stakeholders to evaluate alternative integrity APIs, potentially raising certification costs. Downstream, it could expose the system to new vendor risks if alternatives are not mature.
options: Mandate a comparative assessment of alternative platform integrity providers, requiring each to meet the same security criteria as Apple and Google.
Introduce a phased migration plan that gradually replaces Apple/Google signals with open‑source integrity checks, allowing overlap and risk mitigation.
Establish a joint task force with academia and industry to develop a proprietary, platform‑agnostic integrity framework that can be certified by the Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing supplier diversification against procurement speed; options miss the risk of regulatory delays and lack a concrete fallback schedule.

levers.name: Platform‑Neutral Procurement Framing
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reframes procurement language to require platform neutrality, potentially broadening vendor options but may trigger resistance from incumbent suppliers and extend tender timelines. It could also raise the bar for compliance, requiring more rigorous testing of non‑Apple/Google solutions. Downstream, it may lead to higher upfront costs but improve long‑term resilience.
options: Draft procurement specifications that explicitly prohibit exclusive reliance on Apple or Google platform integrity APIs, mandating alternative verification methods.
Propose a modular procurement approach where core identity services are split into platform‑agnostic components and platform‑dependent extensions.
Advocate for a procurement clause that rewards vendors who provide documented fallback authentication pathways independent of mobile OS ecosystems.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: ensuring procurement neutrality while avoiding vendor lock‑in; options fail to address the challenge of convincing stakeholders that alternative solutions meet existing security standards.

levers.name: Resilience‑Oriented Policy Advocacy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever elevates resilience as a primary policy objective, potentially shifting budget allocations toward contingency planning but may dilute focus on immediate service delivery. It could also create friction with agencies prioritizing cost efficiency. Downstream, it may lead to stronger continuity plans but risk slower adoption of new technologies.
options: Launch a policy brief that quantifies the risk of platform outages and recommends mandatory resilience metrics in all digital identity contracts.
Organize a series of workshops with public sector IT leaders to embed resilience KPIs into procurement evaluation criteria.
Create a cross‑ministerial task force that monitors platform dependency trends and publishes quarterly resilience reports.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: prioritizing resilience over speed; options overlook the need for measurable, enforceable metrics that can be audited by regulators.

levers.name: Technical Demonstrator Credibility
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever provides tangible evidence of fallback viability, potentially accelerating stakeholder buy‑in but may divert resources from policy work. It could also expose technical gaps that require additional development. Downstream, it may increase confidence but also raise expectations for production readiness.
options: Develop a mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS that authenticates via FIDO2/WebAuthn against a simulated identity provider, showcasing platform neutrality.
Build a browser‑based fallback prototype that leverages hardware tokens and OpenID Connect, illustrating non‑OS‑dependent access.
Integrate a hybrid demonstrator that combines mobile and hardware token authentication, demonstrating flexibility across device ecosystems.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing demonstrator depth with resource constraints; options miss the risk of overpromising production readiness and underestimating integration complexity.

levers.name: Stakeholder Coalition Strengthening
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever expands the coalition, potentially increasing influence but may dilute focus and create coordination challenges. It could also lead to conflicting priorities among members. Downstream, it may improve advocacy outcomes but risk slower decision‑making.
options: Invite privacy advocacy groups, academic researchers, and contingency planners to a quarterly steering committee that sets joint advocacy priorities.
Establish a digital identity advisory board that includes representatives from affected ministries, civil society, and industry experts.
Create a public‑facing platform where coalition members can share research findings, policy drafts, and stakeholder feedback in real time.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: broadening coalition versus maintaining strategic focus; options overlook the need for clear decision‑making authority within the coalition.

levers.name: EU Standards Alignment
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever aligns Danish policy with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards, potentially easing cross‑border interoperability but may require adjustments to national procurement rules. It could also expose Denmark to EU regulatory scrutiny. Downstream, it may strengthen Denmark’s position in EU policy but risk delays in national implementation.
options: Submit technical contributions to the European Digital Identity Wallet working group that advocate for platform‑neutral fallback mechanisms.
Draft a national policy brief that maps Danish procurement requirements to EU Digital Identity Wallet interoperability criteria.
Organize a joint EU‑Danish workshop that explores harmonizing fallback authentication standards across member states.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: aligning with EU standards while preserving national autonomy; options miss the challenge of reconciling differing regulatory timelines.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital identity strategy hinges on reducing foreign platform dependence while maintaining service continuity. The selected levers target the core tension between vendor lock‑in and resilience, addressing technical, regulatory, procurement, and stakeholder dimensions. By decoupling platform integrity signals, establishing fallback pathways, restructuring procurement, amplifying engagement, re‑engineering regulation, and modeling cross‑industry resilience, the project creates a multi‑layered pressure system that forces policy change without disrupting existing services. These levers collectively navigate speed, cost, scope, and quality trade‑offs, ensuring that platform neutrality becomes a built‑in requirement rather than an optional add‑on.

levers.name: Platform Integrity Signal Decoupling
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever would shift the trust model from platform‑controlled integrity signals to a self‑contained verification layer, reducing reliance on Apple/Google. This would increase the complexity of the authentication stack and require new certification pathways, potentially delaying rollout. It also opens the door for alternative device‑trust providers, but may fragment the ecosystem if not coordinated.
options: Develop an open‑source device‑trust module that validates hardware integrity without platform APIs, and integrate it into the existing MitID workflow.
Create a cross‑platform integrity verification service that aggregates signals from multiple OS vendors, and embed it as a fallback in the authentication flow.
Establish a hardware‑based attestation enclave that operates independently of OS integrity checks, and expose it through a standardized API for identity providers.
levers.review_lever: Decoupling platform integrity signals reduces vendor lock‑in but introduces certification overhead; the options overlook the need for a unified attestation standard that all stakeholders can adopt.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Pathway Integration
levers.consequences: Implementing a fallback path would provide continuity during platform outages, but it may increase user friction if the fallback requires additional device or credential steps. It also demands new policy language in procurement documents and could slow the adoption of the primary MitID flow if not clearly differentiated.
options: Design a browser‑based WebAuthn fallback that users can access via any modern browser, and publish a step‑by‑step guide for public sector portals.
Introduce a hardware‑token fallback that can be issued to citizens, and integrate token verification into the existing authentication backend.
Implement a short‑lived, QR‑code‑based authentication flow that can be triggered when mobile OS integrity signals are unavailable, and ensure it meets eIDAS security requirements.
levers.review_lever: Providing a fallback path enhances resilience but may dilute user experience; the options fail to address how to incentivize users to adopt the fallback when the primary path is available.

levers.name: Procurement Process Restructuring
levers.consequences: Restructuring procurement to include platform‑neutral clauses would force vendors to design for multiple OS environments, potentially raising development costs. It could also lengthen tender cycles and create uncertainty for suppliers accustomed to a single‑platform focus.
options: Mandate that all future AltID procurement documents require explicit platform‑neutral authentication modules, and audit compliance through a dedicated oversight committee.
Introduce a phased procurement approach where initial pilots test platform‑neutral solutions, and successful vendors receive preferential terms in subsequent full‑scale contracts.
Create a procurement sandbox that allows vendors to demonstrate platform‑neutral capabilities before formal tendering, and publish the sandbox results as a public benchmark.
levers.review_lever: Restructuring procurement promotes neutrality but may increase administrative burden; the options ignore the risk of vendor attrition if the new requirements are perceived as too onerous.

levers.name: Stakeholder Engagement Amplification
levers.consequences: Amplifying stakeholder engagement would broaden the coalition, increasing political pressure on decision makers, but it may also dilute focus and create conflicting priorities. It could also extend the project timeline due to coordination overhead.
options: Organize quarterly cross‑sector workshops that bring together civil society, academia, industry, and government to co‑create platform‑neutral policy drafts.
Launch a public consultation portal that collects structured feedback on fallback authentication scenarios, and use the data to refine technical proposals.
Establish a formal advisory board composed of representatives from each stakeholder group, and convene it monthly to review progress and adjust strategy.
levers.review_lever: Expanding engagement increases legitimacy but risks fragmentation; the options overlook mechanisms to reconcile divergent stakeholder interests into a unified advocacy agenda.

levers.name: Regulatory Framework Re‑engineering
levers.consequences: Re‑engineering the regulatory framework to embed platform neutrality would create a durable policy foundation, but it may trigger extensive legal review cycles and require alignment with EU eIDAS provisions. It could also create uncertainty for existing operators during the transition.
options: Draft a regulatory amendment that defines platform‑neutral authentication as a mandatory requirement for all public digital identity services, and submit it to the Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
Propose a transitional regulatory pathway that allows existing services to adopt platform neutrality over a defined grace period, while mandating new services to comply from inception.
Develop a regulatory sandbox that permits experimental platform‑neutral solutions to operate under provisional approval, and use the results to inform permanent policy.
levers.review_lever: Re‑engineering regulation ensures long‑term change but may stall due to legislative inertia; the options miss the need for a clear enforcement mechanism once the policy is adopted.

levers.name: Cross‑Industry Resilience Modeling
levers.consequences: Modeling resilience across industries would provide evidence of the benefits of platform neutrality, but it may require data sharing agreements that are difficult to secure. It also risks over‑generalizing findings that may not translate directly to the public sector.
options: Conduct a comparative resilience analysis of banking, healthcare, and public administration sectors, quantifying the impact of platform lock‑in on service continuity.
Build a simulation model that projects outage scenarios for mobile OS vendors, and evaluate how fallback authentication mitigates downtime across multiple sectors.
Create a cross‑industry case study repository that documents real‑world incidents where platform dependency caused service disruptions, and use it to advocate for neutrality.
levers.review_lever: Cross‑industry modeling offers persuasive data but may not capture sector‑specific nuances; the options fail to address how to translate findings into actionable procurement language.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Danish digital identity project must balance speed, cost, scope, and quality while confronting entrenched platform lock‑in and procurement inertia. By targeting technical dependencies, legal framing, stakeholder trust, resilience metrics, and alternative notification pathways, the levers collectively address the core tensions of supplier concentration, resilience, and policy influence. Each lever forces a re‑evaluation of assumptions—whether it is the necessity of Apple/Google integrity signals, the feasibility of cross‑platform credentials, or the adequacy of current procurement language—thereby creating a coordinated push toward platform neutrality and a certified fallback authentication path within the 24‑month, 10.5 million DKK budget.

levers.name: Mobile OS Dependency Auditing
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will produce a detailed audit of current mobile OS dependency across all citizen‑facing services, revealing the extent of reliance on Apple and Google integrity signals. The audit will expose hidden contractual clauses and technical lock‑in points, but it may also surface legacy code that cannot be refactored without significant cost, potentially delaying procurement timelines.
options: Conduct a comprehensive code‑level dependency scan across all MitID‑connected applications, mapping every external API call to Apple or Google services, and publish the findings in a public report to pressure vendors.

Engage a third‑party security consultancy to perform a penetration‑testing exercise that specifically targets OS‑level integrity checks, documenting how a single OS policy change could cascade into service outages.

Deploy a lightweight sandbox environment that emulates both iOS and Android integrity APIs, allowing developers to experiment with alternative verification mechanisms without altering production code.
levers.review_lever: Technical dependency mapping highlights OS reliance, yet the options ignore the contractual lock‑in that would require renegotiation of vendor agreements, limiting the audit’s impact.

levers.name: Cross‑Platform Credential Portability
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will push for a design that allows citizen credentials to be stored and verified across multiple operating systems, reducing single‑vendor lock‑in. The shift will increase interoperability but may raise privacy concerns as credentials move between platforms, potentially triggering additional regulatory scrutiny.
options: Standardize on a FIDO2/WebAuthn credential format that can be exported and imported across iOS, Android, and web browsers, and develop a migration toolkit for users.
Introduce a federated credential broker that abstracts platform differences, enabling a single credential to be presented to any service regardless of underlying OS.
Pilot a cross‑platform credential wallet using open‑source hardware tokens, demonstrating that physical tokens can bridge OS gaps without relying on app‑store distribution.
levers.review_lever: The focus on credential portability assumes technical feasibility but neglects the user‑experience friction of migrating credentials, which could erode adoption.

levers.name: Digital Sovereignty Legal Framing
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will reframe the dependency issue as a national sovereignty concern, potentially unlocking legal mechanisms for procurement neutrality. This framing may accelerate policy adoption but could also provoke diplomatic pushback from platform vendors, risking trade‑related retaliation.
options: Draft a legal memorandum that cites Danish constitutional provisions on digital sovereignty to argue for mandatory platform‑neutral procurement clauses in public contracts.
Propose a legislative amendment to the Digital Identity Act that requires any national identity system to include a certified fallback authentication path, citing EU eIDAS obligations.
Engage with the European Parliament to co‑author a resolution that positions Denmark as a leader in digital sovereignty, leveraging EU funding for platform‑neutral research.
levers.review_lever: Legal framing can mobilize policy but may overlook the practical procurement constraints and the need for technical feasibility evidence, limiting its persuasive power.

levers.name: Stakeholder Trust Calibration
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will systematically assess and calibrate trust levels among key stakeholders, ensuring that advocacy efforts are aligned with their risk appetite. The calibration may reveal divergent priorities, forcing the project to negotiate trade‑offs between rapid deployment and thorough security vetting.
options: Conduct a structured stakeholder survey that quantifies trust in platform vendors versus national identity providers, and publish a confidence index to guide policy messaging.
Host a series of moderated workshops where civil‑society groups, privacy advocates, and procurement officials jointly evaluate risk scenarios and rank acceptable fallback mechanisms.
Deploy a real‑time feedback dashboard that tracks stakeholder sentiment during public consultations, allowing the project team to adjust messaging in near‑real time.
levers.review_lever: Trust calibration focuses on perception but may miss the underlying technical dependencies that actually drive risk, limiting its effectiveness in shaping procurement decisions.

levers.name: Resilience Benchmarking Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will establish a set of resilience metrics for Danish digital identity, enabling objective comparison of fallback options. The protocol will raise the bar for future procurements but may also increase the administrative burden on vendors and delay procurement cycles.
options: Define a resilience scorecard that measures uptime, recovery time, and vendor diversity for each authentication pathway, and require vendors to submit scores during tender evaluation.
Create a simulation framework that models service disruption scenarios (e.g., OS policy change, supply chain outage) and evaluates fallback performance against baseline metrics.
Publish a best‑practice guide that outlines resilience testing procedures for identity providers, encouraging voluntary compliance before formal procurement.
levers.review_lever: Benchmarking raises standards but may create a compliance trap where vendors focus on metrics rather than genuine resilience, potentially stifling innovation.

levers.name: Alternative Push Notification Architecture
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will explore push‑notification mechanisms that do not rely on Apple or Google services, reducing platform lock‑in for critical alerts. The new architecture may improve resilience but could increase development complexity and require new security certifications.
options: Design a server‑side push system that uses Web Push API with encrypted payloads, allowing browsers and custom clients to receive alerts without OS‑level mediation.
Implement a lightweight MQTT broker on a trusted national server, enabling device‑to‑device notifications that bypass mobile OS push services.
Prototype a Bluetooth Low Energy beacon network that delivers time‑sensitive notifications to nearby devices, providing an offline fallback when network connectivity is lost.
levers.review_lever: Exploring alternative push channels reduces OS dependence but may overlook the need for end‑to‑end encryption guarantees, risking security gaps in the fallback path.

levers.name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reduces reliance on Apple and Google by diversifying platform integrity signals, but it may increase integration overhead and delay procurement cycles. Establish a joint task force with academia and industry to develop a proprietary, platform‑agnostic integrity framework that can be certified by the Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
levers.review: Core tension: balancing supplier diversification against procurement speed; options miss the risk of regulatory delays and lack a concrete fallback schedule.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Section 106 heritage review for the historic-district alignment triggers a mandatory 45–180-day public comment period that falls entirely outside the project schedule — any opening date committed before permits clear is betting on the minimum review timeline, not the median."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

Project start ASAP

The project involves building demonstrators, engaging with government bodies, and influencing procurement processes, all of which necessitate physical presence and interaction.
